title: Polyolefin nanocomposites formed by melt compounding and transition metal catalyzed ethene homo- and copolymerization in the presence of layered silicates
abstract:
Macromol. Rapid Commun. 20, 423–430 (1999)
423
Polyolefin nanocomposites formed by melt compounding and
transition metal catalyzed ethene homo- and copolymerization in
the presence of layered silicates
Johannes Heinemann, Peter Reichert, Ralf Thomann, Rolf Mu¨lhaupt*
Freiburger Materialforschungszentrum (FMF) und Institut fu¨r Makromolekulare Chemie,
Albert-Ludwigs-Universita¨t, Stefan-Meier-Straße 21, D-79104 Freiburg i.Br., Germany
mulhaupt@fmf.uni-freiburg.de
(Received: April 13, 1999)
SUMMARY: Nanocomposites of high density polyethylene (HDPE), linear low density polyethylene
(LLDPE) and highly branched polyethylene rubbers were prepared both by means of melt compounding and
ethene homo- and copolymerization in the presence of layered silicates which were rendered organophilic via
ion exchange with various quaternary alkyl ammonium cations. In comparison to melt compounding, in-situ
ethene homo- and copolymerization, catalyzed with MAO-activated zirconocene (MBI), nickel (DMN) and
palladium (DMPN) catalysts, proved more effective in nanocomposite formation, as evidenced by larger
interlayer spacings and formation of exfoliated anisotropic nanosilicates with high aspect ratio.
Introduction
Compounds of polyolefins with inorganic materials are
attracting considerable attention in academic and indus-
trial laboratories. On one hand, high surface area silicates
are being applied as supports for various transition metal
compounds such as group IV metallocenes in order to
promote control of morphology development during ole-
fin polymerization1). On the other hand, inorganic fillers
were added during polymerization in order to achieve
improved filler dispersion within the continuous polyole-
fin matrix, especially when using large filler volume frac-
tions nonfusible ultrahigh molecular weight polyethylene
as matrix2). The in-situ polymerization in the presence of
metallocene catalysts was used to prepare aluminium-
flake filled polyethylene with high thermal but low elec-
trical conductivity3, 4). Among various inorganic filler
components, fibers and anisotropic particles with large
aspect ratio have proven to be particularly effective in
polymer matrix reinforcement5). In compounding applica-
tions, nanowhiskers and combinations of nanowhiskers
with microfillers were found to produce poly(propylene)
materials exhibiting the unusual combination of high
stiffness and high impact strength6). However, limited
availability of nanowhiskers, poor nanoparticle dispersion
due to strong interparticle interactions of such high-sur-
face-area nanoparticles, and handling problems relating
to inhalation health hazards have limited commercial
applications.
In order to circumvent such processing problems sev-
eral groups are exploring new synthetic routes aiming at
in-situ
formation
of
anisotropic
nanosilicate
fillers
derived from layered silicates during polymerization or
melt compounding, respectively. This research is stimu-
lated by recent advances in polyamide 6 and polyamide
12
nanocomposite
formation
where
polymerization
occurs in interlayer galleries of organophilic layered sili-
cates7–9). Key inorganic intermediates are layered silicates
such as montmorillonite where individual layers are com-
prised of a central octahedral alumina sheet sandwiched
in between two tetrahedral silica sheets. In hectorites the
central layer is composed of octahedral magnesia. Since
part of the three-valent aluminium ions are replaced by
two-valent magnesium ions, or part of two-valent magne-
sium by lithium, respectively, the individual layers carry
anionic charges which are compensated by incorporating
sodium, calcium or magnesium cations in the interlayer
galleries. In water clay minerals such as sodium bentonite
swell and sodium cations can be exchanged by alkylam-
monium cations. As demonstrated by Hofmann10) during
the 1950’s, this ion exchange renders hydrophilic clay
organophilic and is associated with substantial increases
of the interlayer spacings. When adequate interfacial
adhesion is achieved between polyolefin matrix and orga-
nophilic clays, e.g., by adding maleic anhydride-func-
tional poly(propylene) during melt compounding, poly-
(propylene) nanocomposites are obtained11, 12). Recently,
Jeon and coworkers reported the morphology of HDPE
nanocomposites prepared by solution blending HDPE
with sodium montmorillonite cation-exchanged with pro-
tonated dodecylamine13). According to this study interca-
lation was achieved. However, the presence of fairly large
stacks indicated poor dispersion. Therefore, objective of
this research was to explore nanocomposite formation of
linear and branched polyethylene via in-situ ethene
homo- and copolymerization and to compare in-situ poly-
merization with melt compounding.
Macromol. Rapid Commun. 20, No. 8
i WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69451 Weinheim 1999
1022-1336/99/0808–0423$17.50+.50/0
 424
J. Heinemann, P. Reichert, R. Thomann, R. Mu¨lhaupt
Experimental part
Materials
All manipulations of compounds were carried out by stan-
dard Schlenk, vacuum, and glove box techniques. Toluene
(Fluka) was refluxed and destilled over Na/K alloy prior to
use, 1-octene (Fluka) was refluxed and destilled over CaH2
prior to use. Ethene was obtained from Gerling Holz & Co.
Handels GmbH, Germany, and used without further purifica-
tion. Methylaluminoxane (MAO; 10% in toluene) was
obtained from Witco (Berkamen, Germany). Methanol was
obtained from Riedel-de-Haen and used without further puri-
fication. Synthetic hectorite Optigel SH, purified sodium
bentonites ion-exchanged with dimethyldistearylammonium
and dimethylstearylbenzylammonium ions were obtained
from Su¨d-Chemie AG in Moosburg/Germany. The fluoro-
mica was supplied by CO-OP Chemical Co., Japan. All inor-
ganic components were dried at 608C for the duration of
12 h.
Polymerizations
The
polymerization
procedure
for
the
preparation
of
branched ethene homopolymers using nickel and palladium
diazadiene catalysts was described previously by Schleis and
Heinemann14, 15).
Metallocene-catalyzed
ethene/1-octene
copolymerization was reported in detail by Schneider and
Suhm16, 17). Typically, ethene polymerization catalyzed by
MAO-activated zirconocene dichloride, rac-dimethlysilyle-
nebis(2-methyl-benz[e]indenyl)zirconium dichloride (MBI)
and N,N9-bis(2,6-diisopropylphenyl)-1,4-diaza-2,3-dimethyl-
1,3-butadienenickeldibromide (DMN) catalysts were per-
formed in a 1 L (1.6 L) mechanically stirred glass reactor
(Bu¨chi AG, Ulster/Switzerland). Typically, 400 mL (800
mL) toluene were pumped into the glass reactor. In the case
of (DMPN/borate) as catalyst (see Tab. 1) methylene dichlor-
ide was used. For copolymerization reactions the comono-
mer was injected under argon. After thermal equilibration of
the reactor system ethene was continuously added through a
mass-flow meter (F-111C, Bronkhorst, Nl-7261 AK Ruurlo,
Netherlands) until the reaction mixture was saturated with
ethene. The polymerization was started by adding the calcu-
lated amount of catalyst in 10 mL 1/1 mixture of toluene and
MAO-solution (see Tab. 1 and 2), respectively, equivalent to
[Zr] = 1.25 lmol/L and [Al] = 5 mmol/L. The vapor pressure
of the solvent was considered when calculating the ethene
concentration.
Typically, after 1 h, polymerization
was
quenched by adding 10 mL 2-propanol. The polymer was
precipitated in 1.5 L methanol acidified with 10 mL 10
wt.-% aq. HCl, filtered and dried at 608C in vacuum.
For polymerization reactions in the presence of silicates
the organophilic layered silicate was fed under argon in the
reactor before solvent was injected. The dispersion was
made by stirring in toluene. In some cases the silicate was
dispersed in toluene by using a high shear mixer Ultra Turrax
supplied by Jahnke und Kunkel, IKA Labortechnik in Stau-
fen/Germany. Then this toluene dispersion of the silicate was
injected under argon. The polymer was precipitated in 1.5 L
methanol, filtered and dried at 608C in vacuum.
Characterization
1H NMR spectra were recorded using solutions of 25 mg of
polymer in 0.8 mL C2D2Cl4 at 400 K by means of a Bruker
ARX 300 at 300 MHz; 13C NMR spectra at 75.4 MHz, with a
308 pulse angle, 5 s delay and at least 8000 scans. The sig-
nals
were
referenced
to
C2D2Cl4
(d = 5.90 ppm,
d =
74.06 ppm).
1H NMR spectra for the palladium catalyzed
polyethene were recorded using solutions of 25 mg of poly-
mer in 0.8 mL CDCl3 at 298 K on a Bruker ARX 300 spec-
trometer at 300 MHz; 13C NMR spectra at 75.4 MHz, with a
308 pulse angle, 5 s delay and at least 5000 scans. The sig-
nals
were
referenced
to
CDCl3
(d = 7.24 ppm,
d =
77.00 ppm).
Melting temperatures, Tm, and glass temperatures, Tg,
were determined by means of differential scanning calorime-
try (DSC) with a Perkin Elmer Series 7 from the heating
curve at a heating rate of 10 K/min. Gel permeation chroma-
tography (GPC) was performed on a Knauer Mikrogelset
A14. The degree of branching of polyethylene homo- and
copolymers was investigated as described in literature22).
Melt compounding was performed in a Haake Rheomix 90
twin-screw kneader equipped with a 60 mL mixing chamber
that was preheated at 1908C. After 5 min total mixing time
the samples were quickly recovered and quenched between
metal plates. Sheets of all samples of about 2 mm thickness
were prepared by compression moulding in a press, anneal-
ing at 1708C for 10 min without pressure, for 5 min with 10
bar and cooling under pressure to room temperature in
15 min.
Mechanical properties were measured with an Instrom
4202.
Wide-angle X-ray scattering (WAXS) measurements: The
interlayer distances of the silicate in the in-situ polymerized
nanocomposites and the melt mixed composites were studied
by means of WAXS using a Siemens D500 apparatus with
Cu-Ka radiation (k = 0.15418 nm). The scanning rate was
0.68/min from 2h = 0.88 to 2 h = 128 and 1.28/min from
2h = 128 to 2h = 358.
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements
were performed on a LEO EM 912 Omega apperatus using
an acceleration voltage of 120 kV.
Results and discussions
Polymerization in the presence of layered silicates
Ethene and ethene/1-octene mixtures were polymerized
in the presence of MAO-activated rac-dimethylsilylene-
bis(2-methylbenz[e]indenyl)zirconium dichloride (MBI),
MAO-activated
N,N9-bis(2,6-diisopropylphenyl)-1,4-di-
aza-2,3-dimethyl-1,3-butadienenickel (DMN), and acti-
vator-free [(ArN2C(Me)1C(Me)2NAr)Pd(CH3) (NC1
CH3)]+ BAr94– (DMPN/borate) with Ar equal to 2,6-diiso-
propylphenyl, and Ar9 equal to 3,5-bis(trifluoromethyl)-
phenyl. Ethene polymerization was performed at 408C in
toluene at 6 bar ethene pressure. Polymerization condi-
tions of the individual runs are listed in Tab. 1 and 2. The

Polymerization in the presence of layered silicates
Ethene and ethene/1-octene mixtures were polymerized
in the presence of MAO-activated rac-dimethylsilylene-
bis(2-methylbenz[e]indenyl)zirconium dichloride (MBI),
N,N9-bis(2,6-diisopropylphenyl)-1,4-di-
aza-2,3-dimethyl-1,3-butadienenickel (DMN), and acti-
vator-free [(ArN2C(Me)1C(Me)2NAr)Pd(CH3) (NC1
CH3)]+ BAr94– (DMPN/borate) with Ar equal to 2,6-diiso-
propylphenyl, and Ar9 equal to 3,5-bis(trifluoromethyl)-
phenyl. Ethene polymerization was performed at 408C in
toluene at 6 bar ethene pressure. Polymerization condi-
tions of the individual runs are listed in Tab. 1 and 2. The
Polyolefin nanocomposites formed by melt compounding and transition metal catalyzed ethene ...
catalyst structures are displayed in Scheme 1. The layered
silicate nanocomposites were composed of non-modified
fluoromica (SOM), derived by heating talcum with
Na2SiF6, purified Na-bentonite cation exchanged with
dimethyldistearylammonium (DMDS) cations. A nano-
composite containing non-modified synthetic hectorite
(Optigel), which was tailored to improve dispersion in a
polar medium, was included for comparison. Preferably
the silicate was stirred vigorously in a high shear mixer at
10000 r.p.m. in toluene in order to enhance dispersion of
the layered silicates or swelling organophilic layered sili-
Metallocene catalysts such as MBI are well known to
produce very uniform polyethylene where short-chain
branching can be introduced via addition of 1-octene
comonomer1, 16). In contrast, DMN and DMPN/borate cat-
alysts afford branched ethene homopolymers without
requiring comonomer addition18, 15). As pointed out by
Fink19) and Brookhart18, 20, 21), such branching reactions are
attributed to polyinsertion combined with migration of
the transition metal alkyl along the polyethylene chain
involving repeated sequences of b-hydride elimination
and reinsertion. In contrast to DMN catalyzed ethene
polyethylenes with predominant methyl branches, where
the degree of branching decreased with increasing ethene
pressure, DMPN/borate catalyzed ethene polymerization
yielded completely amorphous, highly branched poly-
ethylenes which were recovered as viscous liquids. While
silicate addition adversely affected catalyst activity for
MBI, both Pd- and Ni-based catalysts were much less
sensitive to addition of non-modified and modified
layered silicates listed in Tab. 1. This finding is in accord

octene in monomer feed) using MBI in the presence and
absence of the purified Na-bentonite cation exchanged
with dimethylstearylbenzylammonium. Reduced catalyst
activity of MBI, as determined by polymer yield referred
to concentration of transition metal and monomer as well
as polymerization time, was mainly attributed to lower
initial polymerization rate, whereas a high and constant
polymerization rate was maintained for prolonged periods
of time. This behavior is typical of modifications with
layered silicates and depends significantly on the types
and contents of layered silicates, silicate modifiers, and
transition metal catalysts. From Tab. 2 it is apparent that
the addition of modified and non-modified layered sili-
cates did not affect either the incorporation of 1-octene
with MBI catalysts, polymer molar masses, or the branch-
ing observed for Ni- and Pd-based catalyst systems. Inter-
estingly, formation of the DMSB nanocomposite by in-
situ ethene/1-octene copolymerization appeared to pre-
vent reactor fouling which was observed for some ethene/
1-octene copolymerization in the absence of silicates (cf.

Properties of polyethylene nanocomposites
In order to study the influence of silicate type, silicate
content, and 1-octene content, nanocomposites were pre-
pared via in-situ polymerization as reported above and
via melt compounding. Melt compounding of linear and
branched polyethylenes with silicates was performed in a
blender for the duration of 5 min at 1908C in the presence
of stabilizers. For comparison always the same silicate
concentration was taken for the melt compounded as for
Visual inspection of melt compounds frequently revealed
inhomogeneities due to inadequate dispersion of the sili-
cates for melt-compounded samples, whereas the corre-
sponding nanocomposites prepared by in-situ polymeriza-
tion were homogeneous and did not contain large silicate
clusters. When 1-octene was added together with ethene
in MBI catalyzed polymerization, the Young’s modulus
decreased with increasing 1-octene content for both filled
and non-filled branched polyethylenes. In the presence of
1-octene comonomer, in-situ polymerization afforded
nanocomposites with improved stiffness and strength, as
strength with respect to those obtained for melt-com-
pounded nanocomposites. Only for HDPE (run PE 145,
Tab. 3) melt compounding gave higher stiffness and

strength without sacrificing high elongation at break. In
general, 1-octene incorporation appeared to improve
compatibility between polyethylene and modified sili-
cates. This was confirmed by means of transmission elec-
tron microscopy (TEM) and wide angle X-ray scattering
(WAXS) studies on the resulting nanocomposites.
Nanocomposite formation, silicate superstructures and
silicate interlayer spacings were examined by means of
wide angle X-ray scattering (WAXS). For high density
polyethylene (Fig. 2) and amorphous poly(ethene-co-1-
octene), containing 10 mol-% 1-octene (Fig. 3), WAXS
traces of the bulk layered silicate modified with dimethyl-

Polyolefin nanocomposites formed by melt compounding and transition metal catalyzed ethene ...
stearylbenzylammonium ions are compared with those of
pure polymer, and polymer nanocomposites (DMSB) pre-
pared by means of melt compounding and in-situ poly-
merization. Interestingly, as apparent from Fig. 2 (trace A
vs. trace B), melt compounding reduced the interlayer
spacing from 1.96 nm to 1.41 nm, reflecting compression
of the silicate layers, most likely due to inadequate com-
patibility. In contrast, in-situ polymerization enhanced
silicate exfoliation as evidenced by the absence of WAXS
signals due to much larger interlayer spacings with
respect to that of bentonite modified with dimethylstear-
ylbenzylammonium ions. Similar behavior was observed
for poly(ethene-co-1-octene) (cf. WAXS trace A for the
modified with dimethylstearylbenzylammo-
nium cations vs. trace B for melt compounded DMSB
and trace C for in-situ polymerization in Fig. 3).
(DMSB), prepared by in-situ polymerization, with respect
to those prepared by melt compounding is clearly visible
in TEM images for polyethylene (Fig. 4) and poly-
(Fig. 5). Fairly large particles, very similar to those of the
pure organophilic bentonite, were detected in melt com-
pounded DMSB, thus indicating poor dispersion and
inadequate exfoliation. In contrast, the in-situ polymeri-
zation yielded much smaller particles with different parti-
cle morphologies, as expected for exfoliated organophilic
bentonites. At low catalyst activities, catalyst residues
form smaller particles, most likely by decomposition of
the MAO activator, which are accumulated at the layered
silicate interface. Both TEM and WAXS studies provide
strong experimental evidence for improved nanocompo-
site formation via the in-situ polymerization route.
Addition of layered silicates such as bentonites and hec-
torites as modified layered silicates, which were rendered
organophilic by means of ion exchange with alkylammo-
nium cations, during ethene homopolymerization, ethene/
1-octene copolymerization and migratory-insertion-type
route to formation of polymeric nanocomposites contain-
ing dispersed nano-scale silicates which are formed in-
situ during polymerization. The presence of n-alkyl
branches, resulting from 1-olefin copolymerization or
migratory insertion polymerization, appears to enhance
compatibility between polyethylene continuous matrix
and dispersed organophilic layered silicates. While non-
modified silicates do not affect catalyst activities, organo-
philic silicates with anionic interlayer charges appear to
interact with cationic transition metal alkyls which are
considered to represent the catalytically active centers. In
comparison to group IV metallocene this adverse effect is

much less pronounced for group VIII based catalysts
which were used for migratory insertion polymerization.
In addition to improvements of mechanical properties,
especially stiffness/strength/toughness/heat distortion bal-
ance, polyolefin nancomposites hold attractive potential
for the development of novel polyolefin materials with
improved barrier properties. The structures of layered
silicates and catalysts can be tailored to achieve improved
polymer properties without sacrificing high catalyst activ-
